name: install
author: dojo-community
context: Install, reinstall, remove, or purge Debian and Ubuntu packages with automation-safe apt-get patterns
info: This skill covers package state changes on Debian-family hosts: installing new packages, reinstalling damaged packages, removing packages while keeping configuration, and purging packages completely. It emphasizes the stable `apt-get` interface for scripts, explicit non-interactive execution, targeted version or release selection, and conservative use of recommends. Agents use it to provision system tools, slim containers, recover from missing binaries, and apply precise package versions without rewriting repository policy.

sections.title: Install and reinstall
sections.body: Run `apt-get update` before installation so candidate metadata is current. Use `apt-get install` to add packages and `apt-get install --reinstall` to repair a package whose files are missing or corrupted. `--no-install-recommends` is the usual automation default when you want a minimal dependency set.

sections.title: Remove vs purge
sections.body: `remove` changes the package state to not-installed but keeps package-managed configuration files. `purge` deletes those configuration files too. Follow removals with `apt-get autoremove --purge` if you also want to clean up orphaned automatically installed dependencies.

sections.title: Selecting versions and target releases
sections.body: APT lets you request an exact package version with `pkg=version` or prefer a distribution or pocket with `-t release` or `pkg/release`. These mechanisms influence dependency resolution and may override the default candidate. Check [[apt/search#policy]] before using them, and use [[apt/pinning]] if the preference should persist.

sections.title: Automation-safe execution
sections.body: Use `apt-get`, not `apt`, for scripts. Set `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive`, pass `-y` only when prompts are expected, and consider `-s` for simulation. This pattern is appropriate for CI, cloud-init, Dockerfiles, and remote shell automation where interactive progress UI is unhelpful or unsafe.

sections.title: Local deb files and dpkg
sections.body: Ubuntu documents `dpkg -i` for local `.deb` files but explicitly cautions that `dpkg` does not resolve dependencies. If the install leaves the host in a broken state, immediately run `apt-get install -f` via [[apt/repair]] so APT can converge the dependency graph again.

links.context: Inspect package names, versions, and dependencies before changing state
Refresh indexes or upgrade the wider system after install work
Recover if a local deb or interrupted install leaves broken dependencies
Persist a package preference instead of using ad hoc target releases
Add a repository if the package is not available from current sources

related.note: Inspect candidates with apt-cache before committing to a package action
Interrupted installs and local debs often need fix-broken or dpkg reconfiguration afterward

scripts.name: Manage package state
scripts.description: Install, reinstall, remove, or purge packages with apt-get

APT_ACTION.description: Package action: install, reinstall, remove, or purge

APT_ASSUME_YES.description: Set to true to pass -y to apt-get

APT_NO_RECOMMENDS.description: Set to true to add --no-install-recommends for installs

APT_TARGET_RELEASE.description: Optional target release passed via -t, such as noble-backports

APT_SKIP_UPDATE.description: Set to true to skip apt-get update before install and reinstall

APT_SIMULATE.description: Set to true to simulate the action with -s

APT_QUIET.description: Set to true to add -q for quieter logging

DEBIAN_FRONTEND.description: Frontend selection for non-interactive package configuration

packages.description: Package specs, optionally including exact versions

targetRelease.description: Optional target release or pocket passed via -t

simulate.description: Run in simulation mode without modifying the system

depends.reason: Package inspection is often useful before selecting a version or target release

body:
# Installing and Removing Packages

Use this node when the package name is already known and the task is to change package state on a Debian or Ubuntu host. For automation, prefer `apt-get` with explicit flags and a non-interactive frontend.

## Install and reinstall

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get -y install curl jq
sudo DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get -y --no-install-recommends install ripgrep fd-find
sudo apt-get install --reinstall ca-certificates
```

APT installs dependencies automatically. If you are installing inside a container or CI image, `--no-install-recommends` often keeps the result smaller and more predictable.

## Remove vs purge

```bash
sudo apt-get remove nginx
sudo apt-get purge nginx
sudo apt-get autoremove --purge
```

`remove` leaves package configuration files behind. `purge` removes package-managed configuration files too. User data in home directories is not touched.

## Selecting versions and releases

```bash
# Exact version
sudo apt-get install openssl=3.0.2-0ubuntu1.20

# Target a suite or pocket
sudo apt-get install -t noble-backports neovim
sudo apt-get install nginx/stable
```

Exact version and target-release selection can downgrade packages or pull from different pockets. Inspect candidates first with [[apt/search#policy]] and understand pinning rules in [[apt/pinning]].

## Non-interactive scripting

```bash
export DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get -y -q install postgresql-client
```

Ubuntu explicitly recommends `apt-get` rather than `apt` inside non-interactive scripts. The surface is more stable and easier to reason about in automation.

## Local .deb files

Ubuntu documents `dpkg -i ./package.deb` for local package files, but warns that `dpkg` alone does not resolve dependencies. If a local install leaves the system inconsistent, use [[apt/repair#fix-broken]] immediately:

```bash
sudo dpkg -i ./vendor-package_1.2.3_amd64.deb
sudo apt-get install -f
```

For repository-backed packages, stay with APT end to end.
